items.q: Is a raga the same thing as a scale?
items.correct: No - a raga includes a set of notes plus rules, characteristic phrases, ornaments and an associated mood, guiding improvisation.
items.misconception: A raga is just the Indian word for a scale.
items.random_wrong: A raga is a kind of drum used in temples.
items.why: Reducing raga to scale misses its prescriptive phrases and expressive identity, which are exactly what performers elaborate in improvisation.

items.q: Do gamelan musicians play from written notation?
items.correct: Traditionally they learn by ear and memory, internalising the cyclic structure and their part's relationship to the core melody.
items.misconception: Gamelan players read from staff notation like an orchestra.
items.random_wrong: Gamelan music is improvised freshly with no fixed pieces at all.
items.why: Gamelan is an aural, communal tradition; understanding rote learning and cyclical structure explains how large ensembles stay precisely together without scores.

items.q: Is African ensemble drumming an unstructured free-for-all?
items.correct: No - each drum has a defined role within interlocking patterns, and a master drummer cues changes with signals.
items.misconception: Everyone just improvises loudly at once with no organisation.
items.random_wrong: Only one drum is ever allowed to play at a time.
items.why: The complexity comes from organised polyrhythm, not chaos; hearing the layered roles and signal phrases is the key listening skill for this music.

items.q: In Indian classical music, a tala is what?
items.why: It's A repeating cycle of beats. “A plucked string instrument” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The gamelan tradition comes from which country?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Indonesia. “Brazil” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In an Indian classical performance, the tanpura provides what?
items.why: Correct answer: The drone. “The main melody” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Several contrasting rhythms sounding at the same time
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Several contrasting rhythms sounding at the same time. “One rhythm played very loudly” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many beats are in one cycle of tintal, the most common tala?
items.why: The real figure is 16 beats. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
